mnemonics.title: Lamport Clock Steps
mnemonics.content: When SENDING: increment first. When RECEIVING: take MAX then increment. Remember: 'Send First, Max Then Add' (SF-MTA)

mnemonics.title: Vector Clock Comparison
mnemonics.content: To compare vector clocks: 'All Less or Equal, One Strictly Less' makes it less than. If neither is less, they're concurrent.

mnemonics.title: Happened-Before Rules
mnemonics.content: Rules: SAME process: order matters; SEND→RECEIVE: causal link; TRANSITIVE: chain continues. Remember: 'Local, Message, Chain' (L-M-C)

mnemonics.title: Total vs Causal Order
mnemonics.content: Causal = respect cause-effect; Total = everyone agrees. Total is STRONGER - all receivers identical. Remember: 'Causal is Cause-Based, Total is Same for All'.

acronyms.usage: Protocol for clock synchronization that reduces but doesn't eliminate physical clock errors
Partial ordering symbol in distributed systems denoting causal precedence
Logical clock that captures causality using array of counters per process
Single counter-based logical clock providing total ordering
Symbol denoting events that are not causally related (neither happened-before the other)
Counter representing number of events process knows have occurred at process i

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline for each process. Show message arrows from send to receive. Label each event with incrementing numbers. Notice how receive events 'catch up' to the max.

Represent each process as a row in a table. When process 0 sends, increment column 0. When receiving from process 1 with vector [2,1,0], take max with local [1,1,0] to get [2,1,0], then increment own.
Draw processes as horizontal lines. Events as dots. Message arrows as diagonal lines. Draw dashed lines to show transitive closure. If you can draw a path from A to B, then A→B.
Place two vector clocks as points in n-dimensional space. If one point is not in the 'northeast' quadrant of the other, they're concurrent. Neither dominates.

analogies.analogy: Like a version number in a document shared between people. Each edit increments the version. When receiving edits, you take the higher version number to stay current.
Like each person maintaining a notebook listing how many edits they've seen from EVERYONE. You know the full context, not just your own count.
Like email timestamps - if you send email A and then email B, A happened-before B. If you reply to an email, your reply happened-after the original.

Causal order is like friends discussing where to meet - if Alice tells Bob and Bob tells Charlie, Charlie knows the plan after Bob. Total order is like a court transcript - everyone gets the same recorded sequence.
